title: CUDA aliases rewrote identifiers inside include paths
summary: The raw exact-alias pass treated angle-bracket header names as code, causing #include <cuComplex.h> to display incorrectly as #include <cf32.h>.
details: The CUDA alias scanner excluded quoted strings and line comments but did not recognize angle-bracket system header paths.
Prefix conceal already had a Tree-sitter literal guard, but exact full-token replacements in aliases.lua bypassed that path-specific protection.
The correction classifies complete include and include_next directives in the shared util.lua skipper before any character-changing row renderer runs.
Backslash-continuation rows inherit include ownership so a header path placed on the following source row also stays verbatim.
The lexical directive fact works for macro and temporarily incomplete includes even when Tree-sitter cannot provide a usable node.
Cursor and Visual reveal continue to preserve source bytes and do not alter the directive classification.

provenance.note: Owner reported the concrete cf32.h mangling and stated that includes should not be touched in general.

provenance.reference: former aliases.lua::cuda_idents raw scan and util.lua::make_skipper
provenance.note: The raw scanner had only string/comment gates, while the shared row skipper had no include ownership fact.

evidence.claim: include, include_next, and continued include paths containing every exact CUDA alias remain display-verbatim while the same tokens transform in code and source bytes remain unchanged.

questions: How are continued includes protected from frontend aliases?
Do library aliases apply inside include paths?

last_verified.method: Reported failure reproduced in the interaction harness, corrected through shared directive ownership, and regression-tested across direct and continued includes.
